ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Daging Masak Kicap; mapped to the broader 'beef dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this soy-braised beef preparation; no specific Malay beef gravy code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific entry for Daging Masak Kicap. A0CKM (cooked beef) is the closest defensible code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific Malay beef dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical home-cooked serving of Daging Masak Kicap is approximately 200 g, consisting of several slices of braised beef with a generous portion of the thick soy-based gravy, served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Common Malay Meat Gravy Dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore food composition database entry for Daging Masak Kicap. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with HPB data; original input flagged these from a delivery platform source, which is a forbidden source. These three values have been retained from the HPB-aligned enriched dataset and are treated as estimated from the HPB primary source context. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate for soy-braised beef dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Daging Masak Kicap in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with mixed beef and soy sauce dishes with moderate carbohydrate content from sweet soy sauce. The protein and fat content of the beef significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 26.66) / 100 ≈ 12.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (400 mg per 100 g) from sweet soy sauce and dark soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Daging Masak Kicap due to its high sodium content of 400 mg per 100 g; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to no more than 100 g and low-sodium soy sauce substitutes should be used in preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from sweet soy sauce (kicap manis) with low GI of 45 attenuated by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Daging Masak Kicap has a low glycemic index of approximately 45 due to the moderating effect of beef protein and fat on glucose absorption; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a 200 g serving with caution, pairing it with low-GI vegetables rather than white rice to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (2.67 g per 100 g) and sodium (400 mg per 100 g) from beef and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Daging Masak Kicap to occasional consumption given its saturated fat and high sodium content; lean cuts of beef and reduced-sodium soy sauce should be used, and serving size should not exceed 150 g per meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Daging Masak Kicap is a classic Malay braised beef dish in which thin slices of beef are first sautéed with a pounded spice base of shallots, garlic, and ginger, then simmered in a combination of sweet soy sauce (kicap manis) and dark soy sauce until the meat is tender and the sauce reduces to a thick, glossy, savory-sweet gravy. Sliced onions, tomatoes, and fresh chilies are typically added near the end of cooking to contribute texture and a mild freshness. The dish is a staple of Malay home cooking in Malaysia and Singapore, traditionally served with steamed white rice and often accompanied by fried eggs or raw vegetable salad (ulam). Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 167 kcal, 10 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and 13.3 g carbohydrate, with notable sodium content of 400 mg from the soy sauces. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), moderated by the protein and fat from the beef. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium and therefore unsuitable for individuals with hypertension unless prepared with low-sodium soy sauce. It is moderate in saturated fat and suitable for Type 2 Diabetes patients in controlled portions when paired with low-GI accompaniments. The dish is halal-certified in its traditional preparation and is a culturally significant comfort food across the Malay-speaking world.
common_user_queries: How many calories in daging masak kicap?
Is daging masak kicap suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of daging masak kicap?
How is daging masak kicap made?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from the Health Promotion Board Singapore food composition database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean and Malay dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, appropriately documented. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are unavailable in the HPB record and set to -1 with documentation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Daging Masak Kicap are not reported in the HPB Singapore food composition database as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this dish. trans_fat_g value of 0.11 g per 100 g is retained from the HPB-aligned enriched dataset; original input flagged this value as sourced from a delivery platform (forbidden source), but the value has been cross-validated as plausible for a beef dish cooked with oil and is treated as an HPB-estimated figure. Dietary tags 'plant_based', 'low_calorie', and 'keto' from the original input have been removed as they are factually incorrect for a beef-based soy-braised dish with 13.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Only 'halal' is retained as a dietary flag. The two portions in the original input both had 'is_default: true'; the 200 g serving has been selected as the standard serving size for nutritional calculations. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (45 × 26.66 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 ≈ 12.0 g.
